critic_raw:
Issue 1: The activation design was opt-in through normal customer-success intake during an employer's active search. Employers who choose to activate a new ranking feature during an active search are a self-selected group — more engaged, more sophisticated about platform use, and likely to have better recruiting outcomes regardless of the feature. Comparing this group to the platform average is not a valid control comparison.

Issue 2: The reporting window runs from early February through May. The marketplace's own context paragraph notes that entry-level demand and candidate responsiveness concentrate around graduation cycles. The February-May window is the graduation-linked seasonal peak for entry-level hiring. Any improvement in interview-to-application rate during this window applies to all employers, not specifically to Smart Shortlist users.

Issue 3: The ATS-integrated employers showed 41% lift versus 22% for dashboard-only employers. The memo attributes this to richer context for the ranking model. But ATS-connected accounts are also described as larger enterprise customers with implementations prioritized over the past year. The integration tier and employer maturity are confounded — the 41% lift reflects both richer context and more sophisticated employer operations.

Issue 4: Feature enablement was staggered by region (West Coast February, Midwest March, Southeast April) and results are reported at an aggregate level. West Coast accounts had three months of exposure by the time Southeast accounts started. Regional hiring cycles also differ. The aggregate 34% lift papers over this heterogeneity.
defender_raw:
The platform adopted interview-to-application rate as the primary marketplace efficiency measure with advisory-board input. This is a stakeholder-validated KPI that reflects whether the marketplace is surfacing candidates worth engaging. Using it is appropriate.

The weekly retraining cycle with a 48-hour label maturation lag is a legitimate data hygiene practice. It is not leakage.

The defender concedes that the activation design (opt-in during active searches) means the treated group is self-selected. This is an observational pilot, not an experiment. The 34% lift is a signal, not a causal estimate.

The defender concedes that the February-May window overlaps with graduation-cycle seasonal dynamics. This is a real confound.

The defender concedes that ATS-integrated employers are also larger enterprise accounts — the integration result is confounded with employer sophistication.

The defender concedes the regional timing issue.

The feature remains commercially promising. A randomized or staged enablement test, or a matched difference-in-differences analysis, would provide cleaner evidence. The pilot result should be treated as a signal worth testing, not as proof of causality.
adjudication_raw: Critic raises 4 issues. Defender concedes all four and argues for treating the pilot as a signal requiring a cleaner test. The defender's rebuttal of the weekly-retraining and KPI attacks is sound. Verdict: empirical_test_agreed.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: employer self-selection in opt-in activation
Issue 2: graduation-season overlap in February-May window
Issue 3: ATS integration tier confounded with employer sophistication
Issue 4: staggered regional enablement with aggregate reporting

empirical_test.condition: Randomized encouragement or staged enablement test, or matched difference-in-differences within same recruiting periods, regions, and integration tiers
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: After balancing employer mix, season, region, and integration tier, interview-to-application improvement near zero or inconsistent
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: Within matched employers in same recruiting windows and activation tiers, randomly enabled accounts show durable lift of at least 10%
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Feature helps a subset of integrated enterprise employers but most of the aggregate gap explained by mix effects
